answer:
**Taqdir** (تقدیر), synonymous with *qismat* (قسمت) meaning fate or predestined lot, appears in the Falsafa corpus's Urdu poetry primarily through English translations as "fate," "destiny," or "written fate." It denotes an inexorable, divinely inscribed order governing human affairs—especially love's separations, life's hardships, and communal paths—often invoked fatalistically yet with ironic defiance or critique. The corpus lacks a systematic treatise but illustrates it vividly in ghazals and nazms by Mirza Ghalib, Sheikh Ibrahim Zauq, and Allama Iqbal.[^1]

### Mirza Ghalib (*Diwan-e-Ghalib*)
Ghalib's lyric self—the archetypal lover (*ʿāshiq*)—positions itself as a hapless victim of *qismat*'s cruelty, lamenting denied union (*visāl*) while paradoxically embracing torment as inevitable. Fate decrees separation or suffering, rendering promises illusory and the beloved's vows fragile; the self endures "constant torment," questioning yet submitting to this cosmic jest.[^2] In another ghazal, the self pities even inanimate objects (*e.g.*, a collar) bound to a lover's fated pain, underscoring *qismat*'s arbitrary tyranny over desire.[^3]

### Sheikh Ibrahim Zauq (*Diwan-e-Zauq*)
Zauq mirrors the classical ghazal stance: the lyric self is sundered from the beloved by "written fate," each soul assigned a separate *qismat*. Positioned in stoic acceptance amid grief, it invokes divine ordinance ("The heavens tore my love from me") without rebellion, equating personal parting to universal transience. The self grieves but affirms fate's decree, seeking no alteration.[^4]

### Allama Iqbal (*Bang-e-Dara*)
Iqbal diversifies the motif, blending fatalism with agency. In a fable, the oppressed cow embodies passive surrender ("What fate has written, we must all obey"), bewailing "wretched destiny" under human dominion; the goat counters, crediting providence (*via* Man) for security, critiquing blind *qismat*-worship.[^5] Elsewhere, the self aligns with exalted destiny: as "Muslim," it claims radiant communal purpose ("faith in the destiny of my Community"), transcending personal sorrow for cosmic victory.[^6] In a moon address, shared "darkened destiny" binds seeker and sought, yet the self asserts purposeful consciousness over mere motion.[^7] Iqbal's persona actively interprets *taqdir* as self-realization or ummah's renewal, not mere lament.

These portrayals reflect Urdu poetry's tension: *taqdir* as divine script (surrender) versus spur to awakening (Iqbal's innovation). Broader corpus philosophy (*e.g.*, smṛti texts) echoes karmic inevitability, but Urdu ghazals personalize it erotically/philosophically.[^8]

[^1]: Searches for "taqdir," "qismat," "fate," and "destiny" yield these Urdu works; see *Bang-E-Dara* Parts 1–3, *Diwan-e-Ghalib*, *Diwan-e-Zauq*.  
[^2]: Mirza Ghalib, *Diwan-E-Ghalib*, Ch. 204 (*Yeh na thi hamari qismat...*), paras. [p-e43078](/works/mirza-ghalib-diwan-e-ghalib-74ed4c/204-yeh-na-thi-hamari-qismat-ke-visaal-e-yaar-hota/translation/#p-e43078), [p-56a7ac](/works/mirza-ghalib-diwan-e-ghalib-74ed4c/204-yeh-na-thi-hamari-qismat-ke-visaal-e-yaar-hota/translation/#p-56a7ac).  
[^3]: Mirza Ghalib, *Diwan-E-Ghalib*, Ch. 16 (*Bask-e dushwaar...*), para. [p-5452df](/works/mirza-ghalib-diwan-e-ghalib-74ed4c/16-bask-e-dushwaar-hai-har-kaam-ka-aasaan-hona/translation/#p-5452df).  
[^4]: Sheikh Ibrahim Zauq, *Diwan-E-Zauq*, Ch. 24 (*Juda hoon yaar se...*), para. [p-23a005](/works/sheikh-ibrahim-zauq-diwan-e-zauq-0fa798/24-juda-hoon-yaar-se-hum-aur-na-ho-raqeeb-juda/translation/#p-23a005).  
[^5]: Allama Iqbal, *Bang-E-Dara Part 1*, Ch. 22 (*Ek Gaye aur Bakri*), paras. [p-153fe5](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-1-55b25c/22-ek-gaye-aur-bakri/translation/#p-153fe5), [p-1dc716](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-1-55b25c/22-ek-gaye-aur-bakri/translation/#p-1dc716).  
[^6]: Allama Iqbal, *Bang-E-Dara Part 3*, Ch. 10 (*Har nafas Iqbal...*), paras. [p-ff38e6](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-3-cc61e8/10-har-nafas-iqbal-tera-aah-mein-mastoor-hai/translation/#p-ff38e6), [p-be7f82](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-3-cc61e8/10-har-nafas-iqbal-tera-aah-mein-mastoor-hai/translation/#p-be7f82).  
[^7]: Allama Iqbal, *Bang-E-Dara Part 1*, Ch. 33 (*Mere*), para. [p-564b95](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-1-55b25c/33-mere/translation/#p-564b95).  
[^8]: *E.g.*, *Āṅgirasa Smṛti*, Ch. 1, on fate's strokes ([p-23850b](/works/unknown-angirasa-smrti-2bf9d1/01/translation/#p-23850b)). No other Urdu poets cover this exhaustively.
